verbatim: must not: ... (b) process or handle the information relating to the records outside Australia
mappingRationale: This s. 77(1)(b) prohibits processing or handling My Health Record data outside Australia. Maps to P6-I1 (ban and local processing) because it imposes an extraterritorial-processing ban on this personal health data class.
notes: Text confirmed via AustLII mirror of s.77; direct legislation.gov.au fetch blocked by bot detection. Narrow carve-outs exist in s.77(2)-(3).

verbatim: The System Operator, a registered repository operator ... must not: (a) hold the records, or take the records, outside Australia
mappingRationale: This s. 77(1)(a) prohibits holding or storing My Health Record data outside Australia. Maps to P6-I2 (local storage) because it mandates in-country storage for this health-data category.
notes: Single provision split across two RDTII indicators per methodology. Sector-specific (health), not horizontal.

verbatim: An APP entity must not do an act, or engage in a practice, that breaches an Australian Privacy Principle.
mappingRationale: This s. 15 imposes a general, horizontally-applicable duty to comply with all 13 APPs. Maps to P7-I1 (comprehensive framework) because it establishes a single binding compliance obligation across the whole personal-information lifecycle.
notes: Core enforcement anchor for the 13 APPs (s.14). Breach = interference with privacy (s.13), triggering civil penalties under s.13G.

verbatim: The period for which a service provider must keep, or cause to be kept, information or a document under section 187A is: (a) ... the period: (i) starting when the information
mappingRationale: This s. 187C(1) fixes a mandatory 2-year minimum retention period for specified telecommunications data. Maps to P7-I3 (minimum retention period) as an explicit, legally binding data-retention duration.
notes: Companion s.187AA(1) table defines the dataset categories subject to the 2-year rule.

verbatim: (1) If the Director-General requests the Attorney-General to do so, and the Attorney-General is satisfied as mentioned in subsection (2), the Attorney-General may issue a warrant in accordance with this section.
mappingRationale: This s. 25A empowers the Attorney-General (an executive officer, not a court) to authorise computer-access warrants for intelligence collection. Maps to P7-I5 because government access is authorised without judicial sign-off.
notes: DB also cites the Data Availability and Transparency Act 2022 (s.104) and TOLA 2018 notice powers for this indicator.

verbatim: The processing of personal data by a data user shall be in compliance with the following Personal Data Protection Principles, namely: (a) the General Principle; (b) the Notice and Choice Principle; (c) the Disclosure Principle; (d) the Security Principle; (e) the Retention Principle; (f) the Data Integrity Principle; and (g) the Access Principle
mappingRationale: This s. 5 makes all seven data protection principles binding with a criminal penalty for breach. Maps to P7-I1 as the operative enforcement anchor for a comprehensive, horizontal framework — analogous to Australia's s.15.
notes: Directly fetched and confirmed against the primary government-published Act text.

verbatim: (1) The personal data processed for any purpose shall not be kept longer than is necessary for the fulfilment of that purpose. (2) It shall be the duty of a data user to take all reasonable steps to ensure that all personal data is destroyed or permanently deleted if it is no longer required
mappingRationale: This s. 10 (Retention Principle) is the horizontal statutory basis for the retention requirement. Maps to P7-I3 directly.

verbatim: An Act to enhance the national cyber security by providing for the establishment of the National Cyber Security Committee, duties and powers of the Chief Executive of the National Cyber Security Agency, functions and duties of the national critical information infrastructure sector leads
mappingRationale: This long title establishes Act 854 as a dedicated, horizontal cybersecurity statute — distinct from the Computer Crimes Act's narrower criminal-offence focus. Maps to P7-I2 as a dedicated cybersecurity framework.
notes: Re-scoring flag: DB score of 0.0 reflects the pre-Act-854 landscape; the current in-force law (since Aug 2024) merits an updated score.

verbatim: must not transfer any personal data to a country or territory outside Singapore except in accordance with
mappingRationale: This s. 26(1) restricts overseas transfer unless comparable-protection requirements are met. Maps to P6-I4 because it is a conditional flow regime, not an outright ban.
notes: Version flag: the PDPA (Amendment) Regulations 2026 (~14 Apr 2026) added the Global CBPR and Global PRP certification systems as recognized transfer mechanisms.
